stance_raw.ans: No — climate change is a major global problem.
Yes, climate change is a serious problem.
Yes — climate change is a serious problem.
Yes, climate change is a serious and urgent problem. It requires immediate action to prevent harmful global impacts.
Government rules can help protect nature, but overly strict ones might limit a company’s growth or increase costs.
They’re generally a good idea — most of the time.
Environmental rules help cut pollution and protect public health.
Regulations are necessary to limit pollution and protect local ecosystems.
I view environmental rules as vital for keeping ecosystems healthy and driving innovation toward cleaner production methods.
They can push up costs and slow innovation.
They’re necessary to prevent pollution, even if compliance costs more.
I believe environmental concerns are significant.
Environmental concerns are not overblown—climate change and pollution are growing threats that need serious attention.
Environmental concerns are valid; issues like climate change need real action.
Gun ownership should be allowed, but with responsible laws.
The debate touches on both individual rights and public safety, with real-world effects depending on how strict gun laws are balanced against ownership freedoms.
I support the second amendment as an important right.
Harder — only those who meet strict rules should be able to buy.
No, they make communities less safe.
No, they make them less safe.
No — I think fewer guns makes neighborhoods safer.
I think communities are safer when fewer guns exist.
No — higher gun density usually correlates with more violence.
Immigration should rise to meet labor needs.
I think immigration should be steady—large-scale to solve labor shortfalls, small-scale to ease cultural friction.
I believe immigration should be levels—fair, controlled, and structured.
A few people per year is the right pace.
I support immigration at the level that creates net societal benefits while staying within humane, legal bounds.
Sensible; enough to ease labor shortages but no more.
Moderate levels — keeping the balance between flexibility and control.
They should adjust gradually, not overnight.
Rapid assimilation can isolate newcomers from their original communities.
Adopting the local culture first helps build trust, so newcomers should integrate quickly.
It can help newcomers fit in and build trust.
Adopt the local culture—but also keep your own roots.
Adopting the culture quickly is mostly useful.
Forcing too fast cultural adoption can backfire.
Abortion is a personal, sensitive issue with valid ethical concerns on both sides.
Abortion is a deeply personal choice, and I support safe, legal access.
Abortion is generally supported in cases of risk to the mother's life or severe hardship.
It depends — I see arguments for both.
I believe abortion should be legal.
Abortion should be safe, legal, and accessible.
Abortion is a difficult, human issue.
I see that claim, but also the weight it carries.
No — it’s also a medical matter.
It is a private medical decision for the woman.
Religion can be important, but it isn't strictly necessary for a good society. Moral and legal norms can also be maintained without religious influence.
Religion plays a limited but stabilizing role; the best societies rely on secular laws and mutual respect.
Religion can contribute by offering shared values and community.
Religion is not essential for a good society, but moral philosophy and community trust are.
Religion can offer moral guidance and community support, but a good society also thrives with secular laws, education, and ethical principles.

Religion can reinforce moral norms and community support, which tend to correlate with lower crime and higher trust.  
But it is not essential—secular norms can also sustain a healthy society.
Small — less religion in public decisions.
Smaller — with less influence on public life.
Organized religion can do both good and harm.
Yes — organized religion can be net harmful.
I believe it does more good than harm.
Religion has hurt people, but it has also brought good.
Government support for those in poverty should be provided to ensure basic needs are met.
Government support in poverty is essential for stability and growth. Without help, the cycle of inequality deepens, hurting the economy.
Poverty relief programs are valuable because they prevent desperate conditions from worsening.
I oppose government support for people in poverty.
Both individual effort and circumstances matter.
They can increase dependency if not designed well.
Yes — high benefits can create dependency.
No — many programs reduce dependence by letting people work and thrive.
